explanation: Theme park rides such as rollercoasters and carnival rides. Carnival game machines. General ambiences to go in AMBIENCE-AMUSEMENT.

discriminators: MACHINES is an ontology of PLACES, not of sounds. The physics under most of these subcategories is identical — an electric motor (a harmonic comb at the shaft rate, plus its pole and blade multiples) driving a periodic mechanical cycle over a broadband bed. DSP can measure that shared morphology precisely, and it usefully prunes every non-machine candidate; it cannot tell you which building the machine was standing in.

Rides are LONG, LOUD, LOW and EVENTFUL, which is why the morphology is 'complex' rather than any single token. A rollercoaster is a whole ARC: a chain-lift clatter (a strongly periodic impulse train at the chain-dog rate — one of the more recognisable periodic signatures anywhere in MACHINES), then a rising wheel roar with a doppler by, then brakes. Several morphologies inside one file.
Carousels and carnival machines add a slow mechanical cycle plus a band organ; pinball and skeeball cabinets are impulsive with bells and solenoids.
UCS routes the crowd away: 'General ambiences to go in AMBIENCE-AMUSEMENT.' If the file is dominated by crowd, screams and midway noise rather than the machinery, it is AMBAmus.
Nothing acoustic marks a ride AS a ride — it is a big machine outdoors. Text ('coaster', 'carousel', 'ferris', 'park', 'ride') decides.

explanation: a bit ambiguous but a very old mechanical machine.

discriminators: MACHINES is an ontology of PLACES, not of sounds. The physics under most of these subcategories is identical — an electric motor (a harmonic comb at the shaft rate, plus its pole and blade multiples) driving a periodic mechanical cycle over a broadband bed. DSP can measure that shared morphology precisely, and it usefully prunes every non-machine candidate; it cannot tell you which building the machine was standing in.

The one thing an antique machine genuinely IS, acoustically: PURELY MECHANICAL, and therefore RHYTHMIC WITHOUT A MOTOR BED. A hand crank, a treadle or a flywheel drives a linkage, so the sound is an almost BARE periodic impulse train — a loom's beater, a sewing machine's needle bar, a press — with very high onset_periodicity and LITTLE OR NO continuous tonal hum underneath.

That ABSENCE of a motor comb is the closest thing to a real discriminator in this subcategory: a modern machine has a hum bed AND a cycle; an antique has only the cycle. LOW harmonicity together with HIGH onset_periodicity leans antique. It is a soft cue, but it is a genuine one.
Everything is metal and wood linkage, so the transients are hard and slightly ringing (high inharmonicity, high crest).
But 'a bit ambiguous' is UCS's own verdict on this subcategory, and AGE IS NOT MEASURABLE. Text ('antique', 'vintage', 'loom', 'treadle', 'linotype', 'mimeograph') decides.
Against COMType: UCS gives typewriters — including antique ones — their own home in COMMUNICATIONS.

explanation: vacuum cleaner... AC and fans have their own category.

discriminators: MACHINES is an ontology of PLACES, not of sounds. The physics under most of these subcategories is identical — an electric motor (a harmonic comb at the shaft rate, plus its pole and blade multiples) driving a periodic mechanical cycle over a broadband bed. DSP can measure that shared morphology precisely, and it usefully prunes every non-machine candidate; it cannot tell you which building the machine was standing in.

The DOMESTIC MOTOR BED: a fridge compressor, a blender, a vacuum, a washing machine. The shared measurable core is a SUSTAINED HARMONIC COMB from an induction motor — a 4-pole 60 Hz motor runs at ~1750 rpm (~29 Hz shaft), with strong radiated energy at 60 and 120 Hz — plus many partials, high stationarity and near-zero spectral_flux.

Many appliances add a slow mechanical CYCLE on top: a washer's agitate/spin phases, a fridge compressor kicking in and out, a toaster's timer and pop. Those are PHASE CHANGES over tens of seconds, not fast onsets, so they raise onset_periodicity only slightly.

This is a grab-bag, and the tier is honestly semantic_only for a reason INTERNAL to the subcategory: a vacuum (loud, noisy, bright) and a fridge (quiet, low, tonal) share almost NOTHING in feature space. No single prior can describe both. The priors below describe their centroid, loosely, and should never over-assert.
UCS carves out the neighbours explicitly: 'AC and fans have their own category' — fans to MACHFan, air conditioning to MACHHvac.
Confusable with MOTRElec by construction: an appliance IS an electric motor in a box.

measured_evidence.selection: clips whose FSD50K labels map unambiguously to this one subcategory (high/medium crosswalk confidence only)

measured_evidence.caveat: Amateur Freesound audio, mono, arbitrary normalization. Treat as a prior on the prior, not as truth. Duration, level, stereo width and band limit are NOT measurable here and are left reasoned.

explanation: A machine for construction that isn't a vehicle or a tool. For example a cement mixer

discriminators: MACHINES is an ontology of PLACES, not of sounds. The physics under most of these subcategories is identical — an electric motor (a harmonic comb at the shaft rate, plus its pole and blade multiples) driving a periodic mechanical cycle over a broadband bed. DSP can measure that shared morphology precisely, and it usefully prunes every non-machine candidate; it cannot tell you which building the machine was standing in.
BIG, LOUD, LOW. Cement mixers, woodchippers, jackhammers, pile drivers, compactors. High absolute level, dominant low_band_energy, long duration.

TWO sub-morphologies live here and both are worth naming, because a single prior cannot cover them: (1) STEADY heavy machines — a mixer drum, a generator — sustained noise over a diesel comb, high stationarity, few onsets; (2) CYCLIC PERCUSSIVE machines — a jackhammer hammering at 15-25 Hz, a pile driver at ~1 Hz — with EXTREMELY high onset_periodicity and an enormous crest factor per blow. A jackhammer is among the most brutally periodic impulse trains in the entire ontology.

UCS's rule is a NEGATIVE and it is purely semantic: 'a machine for construction that ISN'T a vehicle or a tool.' The same engine in a chassis is VEHICLES; in your hands it is TOOLS. No DSP will ever see that distinction.
Against TOOLPneu: a jackhammer is a construction MACHINE here but a pneumatic TOOL there. Follow the text and the implied scale.
Against AMBCnst: a whole job site is ambience; one machine is here.

explanation: Elevator movement and doors would go here.

discriminators: MACHINES is an ontology of PLACES, not of sounds. The physics under most of these subcategories is identical — an electric motor (a harmonic comb at the shaft rate, plus its pole and blade multiples) driving a periodic mechanical cycle over a broadband bed. DSP can measure that shared morphology precisely, and it usefully prunes every non-machine candidate; it cannot tell you which building the machine was standing in.

An elevator is a SCENE, not an event — hence 'complex': [doors open] - [chime] - [doors close with a thunk] - [hoist motor ramps UP] - [steady travel hum with cable and guide-rail rumble] - [motor ramps DOWN] - [levelling] - [doors open]. Several distinct morphologies inside one file, which is exactly why no tight prior fits.

The TRAVEL section is the measurable part, and it has one real signature: a LOW, quiet, tonal hum whose PITCH RISES AND THEN FALLS as the hoist motor accelerates and decelerates. A genuine pitch trajectory with a ramp at each end. Heavy low_band_energy, very low spectral centroid.
Elevator DOORS are routed HERE by UCS rather than to DOORS ('elevator movement AND DOORS would go here'), which makes the subcategory acoustically heterogeneous on purpose.

Against MACHEscl — the sharpest available cue in this pair: an escalator NEVER STOPS and clatters periodically; an elevator STARTS AND STOPS and is otherwise smooth. Look for the ACCELERATION RAMP. Its presence argues elevator; steady periodic clatter argues escalator.
Nothing marks a hoist motor as an ELEVATOR motor. Text decides.

explanation: Escalators and moving walkways would go here.

discriminators: MACHINES is an ontology of PLACES, not of sounds. The physics under most of these subcategories is identical — an electric motor (a harmonic comb at the shaft rate, plus its pole and blade multiples) driving a periodic mechanical cycle over a broadband bed. DSP can measure that shared morphology precisely, and it usefully prunes every non-machine candidate; it cannot tell you which building the machine was standing in.

Acoustically the most CHARACTERISTIC machine in this file, even though it remains semantic_only. An escalator is a CONTINUOUS PERIODIC CLATTER: the step chain engages the drive sprocket at a fixed rate (roughly 1-3 steps/second at a typical 0.5 m/s), producing a metronomic low-frequency clank riding on a steady gearbox hum.

The diagnostic conjunction is unusual and rare: HIGHLY PERIODIC (onset_periodicity ~0.65) AND HIGHLY STATIONARY (~0.8) AT THE SAME TIME. Most periodic things are eventful and most stationary things are onset-free. An escalator is both, because it repeats the same event forever with no start, no stop and no load change.
Against MACHElev: an elevator ACCELERATES and STOPS; an escalator does NEITHER. Look for the ramp.

Against MACHInd (a conveyor): physically the SAME MACHINE — a chain on sprockets, running forever. Genuinely inseparable by DSP; only text ('escalator', 'walkway', 'travelator' versus 'conveyor', 'factory', 'line') separates them.
Moving walkways belong here per UCS.

explanation: ceiling fans. For air conditioning use MACHINES-HVAC.

provenance.reason: the reasoned gate rejected 20% of this subcategory's own true clips

discriminators: MACHINES is an ontology of PLACES, not of sounds. The physics under most of these subcategories is identical — an electric motor (a harmonic comb at the shaft rate, plus its pole and blade multiples) driving a periodic mechanical cycle over a broadband bed. DSP can measure that shared morphology precisely, and it usefully prunes every non-machine candidate; it cannot tell you which building the machine was standing in.

A fan is AERODYNAMIC noise, not mechanical noise — which is precisely why it earns signal_narrowable while most of MACHINES does not. Its morphology is tightly constrained and genuinely measurable: a STATIONARY BROADBAND BED (stationarity > 0.85, spectral_flux near zero, sustain_ratio near 1) with a gently falling spectral slope and NO ONSETS WHATSOEVER. A 'fan' file with transients in it is not a fan.

Riding on that noise is a weak BLADE-PASSING TONE at (rpm / 60) x blade_count — a 5-blade box fan at 1200 rpm gives 100 Hz — plus harmonics. That yields MODEST harmonicity (~0.25) and a small partial_count: enough tonal structure to distinguish a fan from pure static, far too little to call it tonal. The 'a little bit tonal, mostly noise' region is where fans live.
Against COMStatic and AIRHiss: a fan HAS that blade tone and sits at a much lower centroid; static is flatter, brighter and completely toneless.

Against MACHHvac: the SAME PHYSICS at a larger scale — a fan in a duct. UCS splits them by FUNCTION only ('for air conditioning use MACHINES-HVAC'), which is semantic. They will compete on every file; the spectral tilt (see MACHHvac) is the only real lean, and text commits.
UCS explicitly carves fans out of APPLIANCE: 'AC and fans have their own category.'

measured_evidence.caveat: Amateur Freesound audio, mono, arbitrary normalization. Treat as a prior on the prior, not as truth. Duration, level, stereo width and band limit are NOT measurable here and are left reasoned.

explanation: hedge trimmers. See also TOOLS-GARDEN and OBJECTS-GARDEN.

discriminators: MACHINES is an ontology of PLACES, not of sounds. The physics under most of these subcategories is identical — an electric motor (a harmonic comb at the shaft rate, plus its pole and blade multiples) driving a periodic mechanical cycle over a broadband bed. DSP can measure that shared morphology precisely, and it usefully prunes every non-machine candidate; it cannot tell you which building the machine was standing in.

A SMALL SCREAMING ENGINE. Lawnmowers, chainsaws, weed-whackers and leaf blowers are nearly all 2-stroke or small 4-stroke motors running at 3000-10000 rpm. That produces a LOUD, RASPY, RICH HARMONIC COMB — f0 at the firing rate (a 2-stroke at 6000 rpm fires at 100 Hz), a very large partial_count, and heavy distortion. The morphology is genuinely constrained by the physics, so: signal_narrowable.

The characteristic BEHAVIOUR is LOAD MODULATION, and it is measurable: the pitch DROPS when the blade bites (a chainsaw entering wood, a mower hitting thick grass) and RISES when free-revving. An f0 that wanders in anti-correlation with level is an ENGINE UNDER LOAD, and that distinguishes garden machinery from any fixed-speed motor.
Against MOTRComb: physically the SAME OBJECT — a chainsaw IS a combustion motor. A genuine, irreducible collision. Text ('mower', 'chainsaw', 'trimmer', 'hedge', 'lawn', 'blower') decides.

Against TOOLGrdn: UCS splits POWERED landscaping machines (here) from HAND garden tools (rakes, shears, spades). Powered versus manual is measurable IN SPIRIT — a motor comb versus bare impacts — but the routing rule is semantic.
ELECTRIC mowers and blowers BREAK the comb: no firing rate, just a smoother motor whine plus airflow noise. Those look far more like MACHFan and will score there. Expect it.

explanation: all things fitness. See also OBJECTS-GYM

discriminators: MACHINES is an ontology of PLACES, not of sounds. The physics under most of these subcategories is identical — an electric motor (a harmonic comb at the shaft rate, plus its pole and blade multiples) driving a periodic mechanical cycle over a broadband bed. DSP can measure that shared morphology precisely, and it usefully prunes every non-machine candidate; it cannot tell you which building the machine was standing in.

The gym is the purest illustration of WHY most of MACHINES is semantic_only: a rowing machine IS a fan, a treadmill IS a belt motor, a weight stack IS a set of metal impacts. Each one already has a perfectly good home elsewhere in the feature space. The ROOM is the only thing that makes them 'gym', and DSP cannot see rooms.

The shared honest morphology is nonetheless real and worth encoding: HUMAN-PACED PERIODIC MECHANICS. Gym equipment is driven by a BODY, so its cycle sits in the 0.3-2 Hz band (a rowing stroke ~0.5 Hz, a running cadence ~2.7 Hz, a rep ~0.3 Hz) — far SLOWER and far more VARIABLE than industrial machinery. High-ish onset_periodicity at a LOW onset_rate is the family trait.
Weight stacks contribute the loudest events: a plate slamming home is a high-crest metallic impact with a long ring (high inharmonicity).

Against MACHInd — a real and useful cue: a factory machine cycles FASTER and FAR MORE REGULARLY than any human can. Machine-tight periodicity = industrial; loose, drifting periodicity at a body rate = gym. Text commits.
UCS cross-references OBJECTS-GYM for the non-machine gear (dumbbells, mats, ropes).

explanation: maybe Rube Goldberg type machines. See also SCIFI-MACHINE.

discriminators: MACHINES is an ontology of PLACES, not of sounds. The physics under most of these subcategories is identical — an electric motor (a harmonic comb at the shaft rate, plus its pole and blade multiples) driving a periodic mechanical cycle over a broadband bed. DSP can measure that shared morphology precisely, and it usefully prunes every non-machine candidate; it cannot tell you which building the machine was standing in.

HITECH is a FICTIONAL category, and that is the honest finding: 'cool James Bond machines, big elaborate death traps, Rube Goldberg machines' describes a NARRATIVE FUNCTION, not a physical mechanism. These files are almost always DESIGNED — layered servo whines, hydraulic hisses, latch clicks and synthetic tonal elements assembled by a sound designer — never captured from a real object, because no such object exists.

Consequence for this spec: NO gate and NO tight prior is defensible, because there is no referent whose physics could constrain the feature vector. Everything below is broad and low-weight BY INTENT. This is semantic_only in the strongest sense — not 'DSP is too weak to see it' but 'there is nothing there to measure.'

The one weak family trait: designed hi-tech machinery is DENSE and BUSY — many layered elements, high spectral_flux, more high-frequency content than any real motor, and many transients. Real machines are simpler than fictional ones.
UCS points straight at the neighbour: 'See also SCIFI-MACHINE.' In practice SCIMach and MACHTech are the same sound with a different film genre attached to it.
Against MACHMech: HITECH is BIG and elaborate; MECHANISM is SMALL and intricate. Scale is the stated rule, and scale is only weakly audible (via low_band_energy — see MACHMech).

explanation: Air Conditioners. Fans go in MACHINES-FAN. A close up gas furnace flame would go to FIRE-GAS.

discriminators: MACHINES is an ontology of PLACES, not of sounds. The physics under most of these subcategories is identical — an electric motor (a harmonic comb at the shaft rate, plus its pole and blade multiples) driving a periodic mechanical cycle over a broadband bed. DSP can measure that shared morphology precisely, and it usefully prunes every non-machine candidate; it cannot tell you which building the machine was standing in.

HVAC IS A FAN IN A DUCT — and the DUCT is what you can actually hear. Compared with MACHFan, the spectrum is much DARKER and far more LOW-HEAVY (spectral_centroid ~500 Hz versus ~900, a steep negative spectral_slope, low_band_energy > 0.5), because the duct and the building act as a low-pass acoustic filter and the plant is nearly always heard through a wall, a ceiling or a vent. THAT SPECTRAL TILT IS THE ONE REAL, MEASURABLE DIFFERENCE from a fan, and it is why this subcategory earns signal_narrowable rather than semantic_only.

Otherwise it is the ultimate STATIONARY BED: furnaces and air conditioners run at constant load for minutes on end — stationarity ~0.9, spectral_flux ~0.07, effectively no onsets. Compressor units add a low harmonic comb (the motor) and a slow start/stop CYCLE over tens of seconds.

Against MACHFan: same physics, different scale and different filtering. A fan is BRIGHTER and CLOSER; HVAC is DARKER, more rumbling and further away. UCS's own rule is functional ('fans go in MACHINES-FAN'), so text commits.

Against AMBRoom: a room tone often IS the HVAC. If the file is an unattended recording of an empty space, prefer AMBRoom; if the plant itself is the subject, prefer here. This is a framing distinction, not an acoustic one.
UCS routes the flame away: 'a close-up gas furnace flame would go to FIRE-GAS.'

MACHINES is an ontology of PLACES, not of sounds. The physics under most of these subcategories is identical — an electric motor (a harmonic comb at the shaft rate, plus its pole and blade multiples) driving a periodic mechanical cycle over a broadband bed. DSP can measure that shared morphology precisely, and it usefully prunes every non-machine candidate; it cannot tell you which building the machine was standing in.

MACHINE-PACED PERIODICITY. This is the family trait, it is genuinely measurable, and it is the highest onset_periodicity prior in the whole MACHINES file. A factory machine is driven by a camshaft or a PLC, not by a person, so its cycle is RELENTLESSLY REGULAR: a press stamping every 1.2 seconds does it forever, to the millisecond. The inter-onset interval does not drift.

Against MACHGym: the SAME morphology at a HUMAN tempo — and human tempo WANDERS. The axis is REGULARITY AND ITS VARIANCE: machine-tight = industrial, loose and drifting = gym. This is one of the few MACHINES boundaries with any DSP purchase at all.
Layers: a heavy motor and hydraulic bed (low, tonal, sustained) plus a hard periodic mechanical EVENT (a press blow, a punch, a shear) carrying a big metallic ring — high crest, high inharmonicity.

Against MACHCnst: identical physics — heavy machinery under load. The only difference is the BUILDING (a factory versus a job site), which is semantic. Conveyors here are likewise indistinguishable from MACHEscl.
Against AMBInd: if the file is a wide, multi-machine factory BED rather than one machine in focus, it belongs in AMBIENCE-INDUSTRIAL.

explanation: Complex mechanical smaller machines and gadgets
puzzle boxes that open portals to hell. Usually smaller devices.

discriminators: MACHINES is an ontology of PLACES, not of sounds. The physics under most of these subcategories is identical — an electric motor (a harmonic comb at the shaft rate, plus its pole and blade multiples) driving a periodic mechanical cycle over a broadband bed. DSP can measure that shared morphology precisely, and it usefully prunes every non-machine candidate; it cannot tell you which building the machine was standing in.

SMALL is the whole definition — UCS: 'usually smaller devices' — and SMALL IS PARTLY MEASURABLE. Physics obliges: a small mechanism has small, stiff, light parts, so it radiates almost NO LOW FREQUENCY (low_band_energy < 0.2), sits at a HIGH spectral centroid, is QUIET (low RMS, always close-mic'd), and its transients decay FAST. Compare a big industrial press, which is nearly all low end.

So the MACHMech versus MACHInd / MACHTech boundary reduces to a SPECTRAL-TILT-AND-LEVEL test: SMALL = bright, quiet, no bass; BIG = dark, loud, bass-heavy. That is a real axis and a useful one — though far too soft to gate on, since mic distance can fake it.
Morphology is a dense sequence of small clicks, ratchets, gear meshes and latches: many transients, moderately periodic wherever a gear train is involved.

Against MECHANICAL (MECHGear, MECHRtch, MECHLtch, MECHClik): those subcategories are the COMPONENT EVENTS; MACHMech is the WHOLE GADGET doing its whole thing. If the file is a single latch or a single ratchet, prefer MECHANICAL.
'Puzzle boxes that open portals to hell' — like MACHTech, a good deal of this is DESIGNED rather than captured, which caps how tight any prior can honestly be.

explanation: Machines typically found in a hospital

discriminators: MACHINES is an ontology of PLACES, not of sounds. The physics under most of these subcategories is identical — an electric motor (a harmonic comb at the shaft rate, plus its pole and blade multiples) driving a periodic mechanical cycle over a broadband bed. DSP can measure that shared morphology precisely, and it usefully prunes every non-machine candidate; it cannot tell you which building the machine was standing in.

'Machines typically found in a hospital' — UCS's definition is LITERALLY A ROOM. This is the canonical semantic_only case and no amount of DSP will ever fix it: a dialysis pump is a pump, a centrifuge is a motor, an infusion pump is a tiny stepper. Nothing about the waveform says 'hospital'.

The one real family trait, and it is a good one: medical machines are QUIET, SLOW and PERIODIC AT A BODY RATE. Ventilators cycle at 0.2-0.3 Hz (12-18 breaths/minute), infusion pumps tick every few seconds, monitors beep at the heart rate (~1 Hz). A very-low-rate, VERY-REGULAR onset train at LOW level is a genuine hint — that 1 Hz metronome is the sound of a hospital in every film ever made.

MRI is the violent exception and is genuinely separable from everything: gradient coils produce EXTREMELY loud, harsh, square-wave-like buzzing at fixed pulse-sequence rates (hundreds of Hz up to a few kHz) with brutal periodicity and near-clipping levels. A deafening periodic buzz is a real MRI candidate and looks like nothing else in MACHINES.
UCS routes the beeps away: monitor tones belong in BEEPS-MEDICAL. Keep the MACHINE here.
Confusable with MACHPump (infusion and dialysis pumps ARE pumps) and MACHOffc (a centrifuge sounds exactly like a printer motor).

explanation: Machine sounds not fitting another category in this list. See also MOTOR category.

discriminators: MACHINES is an ontology of PLACES, not of sounds. The physics under most of these subcategories is identical — an electric motor (a harmonic comb at the shaft rate, plus its pole and blade multiples) driving a periodic mechanical cycle over a broadband bed. DSP can measure that shared morphology precisely, and it usefully prunes every non-machine candidate; it cannot tell you which building the machine was standing in.
The MACHINES catch-all. Deliberately un-gated: with sixteen siblings, gate collisions are frequent, and MISC must stay reachable as the abstention target.

UCS points at the neighbour: 'See also MOTOR category.' The MACHINES/MOTORS boundary is one of the few in this file that IS measurable, and it is worth stating: a MOTOR is a bare harmonic comb with NO mechanical cycle on top (onset_periodicity ~0, no onsets); a MACHINE is a motor that is DOING something (onset_periodicity > 0, a periodic mechanical event riding the comb). If the file is just the comb, send it to MOTORS.

explanation: Machines typically found in an office
such as printers and copy machines.

discriminators: MACHINES is an ontology of PLACES, not of sounds. The physics under most of these subcategories is identical — an electric motor (a harmonic comb at the shaft rate, plus its pole and blade multiples) driving a periodic mechanical cycle over a broadband bed. DSP can measure that shared morphology precisely, and it usefully prunes every non-machine candidate; it cannot tell you which building the machine was standing in.

Printers, copiers, scanners, shredders, fax machines. The shared morphology is a PAPER TRANSPORT: a stepper or servo motor driving rollers, so the sound is a HIGH-PITCHED, QUIET, PERIODIC WHIR with a strongly periodic mechanical cycle and almost NO low end (low_band_energy < 0.25). STEPPER motors are especially characteristic — their step rate produces an audible buzzy tone that SLIDES in pitch as the carriage accelerates and decelerates. A pitch-sliding buzz is a stepper, and steppers live in office machines.

A print job is a SCENE: [warm-up whir] - [paper feed grab] - [carriage pass, once per line, metronomically] - [eject] - [fan spin-down]. The per-line carriage pass is a beautifully regular onset train, which is why onset_periodicity leads the priors.
But 'typically found in an office' is a ROOM definition — semantic_only, exactly like MACHMed. A copier's motor and a centrifuge's motor are the SAME MOTOR.
UCS sends printers HERE from COMPUTERS explicitly: 'Printers would go to MACHINES-OFFICE.'

Against COMType and CMPTKey: a typewriter and a keyboard are impulse trains from FINGER strikes; office machines are MOTOR-DRIVEN transports. The presence of a continuous motor bed UNDER the impulses is the tell.
Against AMBOffc: a whole office bed (phones, chatter, HVAC) is ambience, not a machine.

measured_evidence.caveat: Amateur Freesound audio, mono, arbitrary normalization. Treat as a prior on the prior, not as truth. Duration, level, stereo width and band limit are NOT measurable here and are left reasoned.

explanation: sump pumps. Air compressors would fall into this category.

discriminators: MACHINES is an ontology of PLACES, not of sounds. The physics under most of these subcategories is identical — an electric motor (a harmonic comb at the shaft rate, plus its pole and blade multiples) driving a periodic mechanical cycle over a broadband bed. DSP can measure that shared morphology precisely, and it usefully prunes every non-machine candidate; it cannot tell you which building the machine was standing in.

A PUMP IS A CLOCK. This is the most PERIODIC subcategory in MACHINES and the reason it earns signal_narrowable: a reciprocating pump or compressor is a PISTON, so it emits an unmissable RECIPROCATION at a fixed rate. onset_periodicity ~0.8 is the highest prior in this file, and it is honest. An aquarium pump ticks at 1-3 Hz; a compressor's motor cycles at 20-60 Hz.

The full picture is TWO PERIODICITIES AT ONCE, and that is quite characteristic: a fast MOTOR comb (harmonic, tens to hundreds of Hz) AMPLITUDE-MODULATED by a slow MECHANICAL stroke (a few Hz). A PULSING HUM. Any record showing strong low-frequency AM imposed on a harmonic carrier is a strong pump candidate.

CENTRIFUGAL and submersible pumps BREAK the rule: they are smooth and continuous, with no reciprocation at all, and they then look far more like MACHFan or MACHHvac. Expect those to compete and do not over-trust the periodicity prior on them.
Against MACHHvac: an HVAC unit is a STEADY bed; a pump PULSES. onset_periodicity is the axis, and it works.
Air compressors belong here per UCS ('air compressors would fall into this category'), which places them right next to TOOLPneu — the compressor is the machine; the tool it feeds is not.
